sentences.text: No.
It was security, actually.
I didn't like it because, first of all, I felt some, under the surface, some kind of anti-Semitic push.
This is one.
And the supervision of the Communist Russians, you know, the Poles were totally under their supervision.
This is one.
And I-- the only thing what I had the satisfaction-- that I was giving the security information from those dossiers to take care of those people who were against the Jews and against the Poles.
And they really took care of those very much.
And-- but I didn't like-- but the border was still green.
People used to come to my city and so forth.
So one day, Mr.
Lipfeld ?], who lives now in Canada, and [?
Lichter ?], another friend of mine, came to me to visit.
OK?
And I told them like, you know, straight out, I said, I don't want to be here.
The only way I have to quit here-- and I want to go-- They were in West Germany, in Munich, at that time.
I said, you know what you do, why don't you make me documents there, bring it to me, and I go back with you?
And they did.
They brought documents, made up some place there, that I live in Munich, and I was there.
And there and there and there.
Had the pogrom happened yet?
No.
No.
Pogrom was '46.
I'm talking about '45, 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I'm talking about now almost December, November '45, or something like this.
So they brought me.
I took the pistol that I carried.
I threw it in the water, in the lagoon.
I put on civilian clothes, and we started to go.
We started to get out of my city.
We went to Czechoslovakia.
From Czechoslovakia, we went to Austria.
From Austria, we went to Leipzig, to-- to Germany.
It was the eastern part of Germany.
We got caught by the Russian police or whoever.
They did arrest us, put us in the basement.
I spoke Russian.
And I talked to them.
And I tell them a lot of stories.
They gave [? out ?] to eat, and they said, [RUSSIAN], go.
And they let us go.
They let us go, and we came to Munich, Germany.
I came to Munich, Germany, and I stayed with the-- because the-- with those friends.
And there was a concert once or-- or-- a some kind of a-- I don't know what it was-- some people was talking about the Holocaust, about the-- the concentration camps in a big place.
And I had some documents with my [INAUDIBLE], with my-- from school, papers from school, and pictures of my family, and some money here.
And after the concert, I see nothing is there.
Somebody stole it.
I was heartbroken.
I was so angry because only Jewish people were there.
There were nobody else.
That somebody stole it-- that I was ready to go back to Poland.
And I was thinking.
I asked Mr.
Lipfeld, ?] go and make contact for me with those-- those people from the mafia there in Munich, those pickpockets.
And let-- what do they do?
Let them take the money that I have, but let them give me back the documents and so forth.
Nothing.
I was left without documents, without anything.
But I decided to stay.
So I went to [?
Unrah. ?] The [?
Unrah ?] had a special settlement place in Deutsches Museum in a big place.
And they make some [? bad ?] cards for people, for, you know, DPs.
They used to call us displayed-- displayed persons.
And they started to give some lectures.
There were some professors from Lithuania, Russia-- you know-- you know, college studies.
So I registered.
And they used to give us to eat, too.
And I started to get some medical education.

And after that, when the German university, Ludwig Maximilian University, opened, I matriculated myself and started to study at the university, at the medical faculty of the university, of Maximilian University.
And I studied.
And I assigned me to a-- I didn't have to pay tuition.
OK?
And the joint distribution committee helped us-- us with some supplies.
I used to go at 4 o'clock in the morning to a special DP camp, where I was assigned for-- to, and collect food for a few people, for us, for these students, and bring it and distribute.
And then I was assigned to a German lady, to her apartment.
They gave me a room there.
The husband was working before in a IRS department, from-- let's say in some-- And he was not-- he was a Nazi, but they used to call [NON-ENGLISH].
He was nothing--
He would go along.
Go along, yes.
He wasn't an instigator.
Yes.
OK.
They had no children.
They had a dog and a canary, and his sister used to live there.
They-- those people took care of me like I would be their son.
Not because they were afraid of me, but really, they-- they really, they-- she did anything-- wash my clothes, wash my hair.
She did anything like I would be a really found son someplace.
Really incredible.
Gemeiner, G-E-M-E-I-N-E-R.
They're dead already.
He died, and she died.
I was in contact with them.
In fact, in 1972, I went to the Olympics when-- you know when they killed the Israelis there.
And I stayed by her.
She let me go to a hotel.
And-- and I-- you know, we-- I wrote letters to her.
You k now, we were in contact and so forth.
And then I met a young lady, a very nice lady by the name of Hanka [?
Rinksi, ?] who I started to date.
I meet her-- I meet her some place.
Once I met her, I think, by-- by a-- not rail, but tram.
You know?
And I-- I was invalid, so I had a special kind of a-- I could sit in the front, in the hall there, in the front seat.
And some other people had to sit back.
And once she was sitting there, I was sitting, and she asked me to let her go there.
And I said something.
She liked it, or not liked it, but she liked me.
And it started that way, you see.
We met, and so forth.
She was a beautiful girl.
She is still, but she was then beautiful, young.
And we started to date, and I helped her out.
She studied dentistry.
And she lived in another apartment not far from me by [INAUDIBLE].
I helped out in studies a little bit, not too much, but a little bit, the studies.
And 1949, March the 20th, we got married.
After a courtship of--
A courtship of-- since about 1945, '46.
And-- yeah, maybe '47, beginning of '47.
It was about a year and a half or something like that.
[INAUDIBLE] And her parents survived because they came from Russia.
They left United States in-- in April, around Passover, to Milwaukee because of the beer connection.
They send them to Milwaukee.
Was-- but-- he had to do with beer-- you know, he--
--like a brew-- Yeah, uh-huh.
So they send him because Milwaukee was a place for beer.
So-- but I went originally.
I had to wait because May 20, I passed my examination as a physician, and she passed as a dentist.
OK?
You had to write a dissertation.
I helped out a little bit for the doctor.
You see, you did graduate as a physician, but not as doctor.
For a doctor, you had to make a doctorate, a dissertation.
You see?
So when we finished this, we started to make ways of going back to United States.
Actually, they wanted to go to Israel.
I didn't want to go to Israel.
Why?
Because I thought, I am not aggressive enough to-- to go to Israel.
Because after my-- well, maybe I'm exaggerating a bit-- because after I lived in the ghetto, no-- the ghetto, you-- not-- the ghetto was not what you knew-- what you do, but who you knew.
You know, you had always to have [? a pull. ?] And I'm a little maybe shy, a little bit, or something like that.
I don't know.
So I didn't want to go there.
I wanted to go to New Zealand, actually.
Right.
Yeah.
Those were extraordinary times.
I think the extraordinary times bring something out from you, which normally you don't have it.
No.
I was strong, and I-- I-- nobody could tell me anything.
You know?
Like, the-- the-- one of the policeman, the [? spiegel ?], he told me something, and I fought-- fought with him, the chief of police.
I fought for-- you know.
That's all, you see?
Nobody could do anything to me.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
This is no problem, no problem at all, you see?
And we came to United States, and, you know, when we came at 2 o'clock in the morning, it was so hot.
And a lot of people were sick, sitting outside.
And it was-- we saw the Statue of Liberty, and I said, OK, I am in the land of the free.
And I knew about democracy.
I knew about United States and everything.
Army transporter, General [INAUDIBLE].
A-- a-- what its name?
Kaiser ship, one of the ships made, the transporters.
And we went through Halifax where the North Atlantic was a very stormy ocean.
People-- it was raining.
People were sitting on the deck with-- deck-- covered like that, sick totally.
I was distributing Dramamine, but my wife worked in the-- in the-- they had a beautiful, not even open, dental cabinet.
And she used to work there for the-- it was a civilian crew.
She used to get $0.50, $1 for each guy what she did.
And we used to use the money to put-- there was a machine for Coke-- a Coke.
And-- so I didn't like the Coke, but I like the seltzer water.
So I used to put specially this paper cup.
You see, that's the way that I wouldn't get the Coke, but only the water.
And that's the way.
And we used to get all of their special sandwiches, which other people didn't get.
And we came to New York.
And in New York, we put us the [? highest. ?] Put us in a-- on the Second Avenue.
There was a special place with cubicles.
And I had some friends already.
They were working in a hospital there, King David Hospital.
They said, come on with me, with us.
You know, go to this administrator, and maybe you will work.
So I went there.
He said, OK, go in an ambulance.
We used to collect all the drunks and narcotic addicts on the street.
And I got sick, very sick.
What year was this?
That was in 1950.
We came here August 20, 1950.
And I did it for a while, got very sick.
They took me emergency to [INAUDIBLE] hospital, to contagious hospital.
I got mumps, even though I had mumps as a child.
I got again, mumps-- mumps, pneumonia, and [INAUDIBLE], you know, the testicles.
Very bad.
I was very sick.
And I was so despondent then, after I got out, that I said, let's go to Milwaukee.
And we left.
We went to Milwaukee.
Although, they gave us an apartment there by a lady, later on, you see, we had a very nice place, but I said, no.
I want to go to Milwaukee.
And in Milwaukee, we went again to the Jewish Federation.
They said, you have a diploma?
I said, yes.
Why don't you go to Mount Sinai and be an intern?
I didn't speak any English, not even a word of English.
So I sat there.
There was Dr.
Rosenzweig, he was the administrator of the hospital.
And he said, you know what, you have a diploma.
It's very good, and you made it with cum laude, and so forth.
He said, you know what, go be an intern, go to seventh floor, the people having babies, the women having babies.
They yell all the time.
They don't talk.
You don't have to talk at all.
There's a special form.
You say yes or no, and that's all.
So I went there, and I didn't sleep all night.
And I talked to the nurses, and I picked up.
In six weeks, I could talk.
And I was a good intern.
I spent-- I was supposed to be three months on OB.
I spent four months on OB.
And there was a short guy, Dr.
Kriger ?], who took special care of me.
And this Dr.
Kriger ?] was the most outstanding obstetrician in Milwaukee.
He took me there, told me how to deliver, how to do everything.
And he said, you have the stamina to be an obstetrician.
Yeah.
You have to be in-- you don't sleep nights.
You make-- have to make decisions immediately.
You see, you have to deal with two lives, you know.
You deal with healthy people.
I mean, the mother and the child are healthy.
You know, if you are internist, you deal with sick people.
If you are, let's say, a rheumatologist, you understand what [INAUDIBLE].
Here you deal, and the-- and the product has to be also, healthy.
And the decision has to be immediate.
You cannot wait too long because the baby goes or something else happens.
So that's where I started.
And I spent there a year, and I-- after a year, in 1951, they didn't give me the license in Milwaukee.
Wisconsin did not recognize the university.
Even they registered me for selective service from Wisconsin.
I went to Chicago.
I stayed for about a week in-- in-- in YMCA on-- on Wabash-- Wabash there.
Stayed there, passed the boards.
They gave me a dictionary, and I passed, first time, the boards.
I got my license.
I got my license, and I went to-- to Michael Reese for residency.
They called me to the Army, Korean War.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From my residence, went to the Korean War.
They took me to Korea.
I spent 16 months in Korea.
As a physician, yes.
A physician.
First I had a-- was battalion aid station, you know?
Then I was division artillery surgeon of the 25th Division.
Then I was in the MASH.
Then I had 16 points-- 16-- 16 months.
They wanted to take me to general-- general hospital in Tokyo, if I would extend my tour of duty for another six months.
I had already a child.
My son was born.
I decided not.
I asked for the 5th Army headquarters area, and they sent me here to Fort Leavenworth, Missouri for six months.
And when I finished the tour of duty, I started my-- I worked for a year-- because I had a family-- in a clinic because I had a license, and started my residency at Mount Sinai, here in Chicago.
I was not a-- I was not a-- a citizen.
I was-- I was naturalized in Seoul, Korea by-- by a judge from Hawaii.
And the judge in Hawaii didn't let me get rid of the Bronislaw.
I wanted to get rid, completely, of the name.
I didn't need it.
So I have to use it as a 'b,' as a middle initial, but on my papers, it is written on the back that my name originally was Bronislaw.
So that's, you know--
Right.
Well, what can you do?
If they call, they call.
You see.
The only thing, I spoke perfect Russian, I spoke German, I spoke Polish.
They sent me to Korea instead to Europe, you see, where I could do much more better.
You know.
They send me over there.
But they were panic-- panicky at the time.
No.
A few words, only.
You know.
It was very-- it was a very, very primitive country at that time because, you know, Japanese occupation.
They really exploited the country very, very bad.
Now it's completely different.
And when I-- when I passed the license, and I settled in Chicago-- Chicago, went on the south side, where my friend used to have a practice.
He wrote me I should go with him in general practice, but I had already 16 months of my residency, [INAUDIBLE].
And I said, I invested already that much time.
And I finished my residency, and I settled on the south side there.
And there was about 250 Jewish families.
We had a temple, a rabbi, and so forth, you see?
But they dissipated in Scottsdale, and the community dissipated.
And I worked myself up to be a consultant.
I was chairman of the department for three years.
I was-- At Michael Reese?
No, no, at the Rush Medical School, Rush [INAUDIBLE].
And I was a consultant.
And-- and I'm ready to retire now.
I'm ready to-- to-- July 1, another three weeks or, what, 20 days, three Mondays-- to finish my career as a physician.
Well, first of all, I have to see what happens.
I taught all my life.
And I think I have to do-- I-- I have some hobbies.
I write.
I wrote some poetry.
I-- maybe I will hone it a little bit better yet, you see, because I will have time.
I could never read anything besides medicine.
OK?
This way I will be able to read whatever I want to.
I have four grandchildren.
I did neglect, to a large extent, my family because I was a solo practitioner, day and night, you see, work and so fort.
It was a very difficult-- but I did have no basis.
My wife's parents were poor.
They couldn't help me, financially.
I had to build up some kind of a life for the future, and it was hard.
But we made it.
And I'm serene now and happy.
And I look back on all those years with a look, which it's long kind of a view.
And I think that I did serve the society well.
And I think that I brought some values to the United States, you see?
No.
We're going to go another half a tape.
No.
I can only say something about my family, which maybe I glanced over a little bit.
My grand, grandfather, on my mother's side, his name was [?
Schmuel ?] Novak, N-O-V-A-K.
This man did cooperate with the Russians.
You see, Poland was under Russian occupation with Germany and Austria.
It was split in the 1700s.
And in 1863, the Poles made an insurrection.
It so happened that he cooperated with the Russians.
So he got a-- went to-- supposedly to Petersburg, Saint Petersburg, was invited to the czar.
That was the legend of the family.
And over there, he got the document that [?
Schmuel ?] Novak is full of confidence of the czar, of the crown.
And for this, they gave him a lot of land [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and also a woodworking factory.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- the family told me about it.
This was one, my grand, grandfather.
My mother's-- my-- this was the-- the father of my uncle's-- my grandfather's wife, this-- [?
Kaminski, ?] which was the president of the-- of the Jewish Federation.
My father's grand, grandfather used to be called Moshe [?
Rofe ?].
Rofe, ?] in Hebrew, is a-- a-- a doctor, a healer.
Well, I don't think he was a healer, but they used to call him like that.
And was-- those are two legends of the family that I-- that carry with me, and I knew about it.
I don't know.
As a child, I didn't pay too much attention to the extended family, as far as who, where, and so forth.
I knew only the uncles, and I knew my cousins, and with my parents, because I think it was different than here.
Here it's a bit different.
In Poland, on the old country, your people used to live together in the homes and so forth.
But by us, we were a little more assimilated, you see.
Each was on his own, like, you know, one was a dentist, one was a lawyer, one was this.
So it was not-- a-- we didn't live in one place, and we didn't live in a village like in a shtetl.
Our town was over 80,000 people, and out of the 80,000 in 1940 were 26,000 Jewish people.
And we had societies there with high cultural development and possibilities.
And it was a little bit different.
At the end of the war there were two Jews left in your town.
Yes.
One Jew was actually-- he was not a Jew.
He was a [? cadera. ?] He was a converted Jew.
I think he's still alive.
No.
No.
I-- the pogrom-- I escaped from there by the end of 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The pogrom happened July 4, 1946.
When I was in the security working, I used to frequent the Planty 7 where about 200 Jews used to stay there.
Yes, yes, on the river.
The river, Silnica, was a river bisecting our city in-- in-- in two parts.
And I used to, some way, give them the security to some extent, you see, help them out, and so forth.
Some of the people had guns there.
But when I left it was-- I left about, what, seven months before the pogrom.
The pogrom started that way-- a child supposedly was lost someplace, a boy.
They couldn't find him.
Finally, he did come out to the surface, and he said that some people took him in a basement of a building.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they beat him up and did something to him.
So the parents, before, started to look for him.
They went to the-- to the security.
Nobody could find him.
What actually happened, he went to a village by himself and was afraid to tell the parents.

So the woman, the mother, the woman started to say and yell out that the Jews who are on Planty 7 tried to kill-- and they killed probably some other Polish Christian children-- to get the blood for the matzah OK?
Or to drink the blood because they came from the concentration camps, and they need to-- to be healthy, or something to-- to make them seem little more stronger.
And they primitive people and some other people who wanted to do that because it was still a revolution at that time.
The regime, which came from Russia and took over the-- the government in Poland, was anti-capitalistic.
It was under Stalin.
OK?
And there was a lot of partisans from before who were anti-Communists.
And even the Polish, the-- the people who were from the London government, with Mikolaj and so forth, you see, were against the Russians.
OK?
So there was a lot yet.
It was a-- was like a civil war at that time.
The regime didn't set in yet.
OK?
So the people started to aggregate by this building.
And they started to some-- agitate that those Jews there, you see, they are rich, and they are supported, and they take care of this [? way ?] of the children.
And they started to attack.
They even involved the members of the militia, of the people who were supposed to defend them.
And when they tried to intervene, Mr.
Kahana, who was the president of the organization there, went, tried to intervene, and tried to get the-- the bishop of Kielce to intervene.
He couldn't accomplish anything.
OK?
He didn't want to have a part of it because, you know, they were against the Communists, too.
See, there was still an antagonistic way of doing it.

And what happened, finally, when they brought the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the fire department to disperse the people with fire-- with the water hoses, they cut the hoses, the people, and just broke the door in.
And they start to-- the mob got in.
They started to throw out the people through the windows, kill them with any form of-- of-- you know, with sticks and-- and axes and any kind of implementation.
And they killed a lot of-- even a woman with a child.
So they ask-- I have the court proceedings.
The asked the man, why did you kill the child?
[INAUDIBLE] He said, what would the child do without the mother?
The man says, so this is why I had to kill the baby, too.
And the atrocity was very bad.
And they killed about 42-- 46 people.
My aunt was there, but at that time, she was not in the building.
She was by her sister.

And they took the people, and they-- the-- the wounded-- there were a lot of wounded people-- they took them to Lodz, to a special hospital because they were even afraid to take them locally to the hospital in my city there, in Kielce.
Because they didn't believe.
They took people from their train, Jewish people.
And when the train was going, they threw them out of the trains.
It happened not only in Kielce.
It happened in Poland, in all Poland.
No.
I didn't.
